Joshua dismissed the tribes with good advice. Those who have a commandment have it in vain unless they do the commandment; and it will not be done correctly unless we pay close attention. In particular, to love the Lord our God, as the best of beings, and the best of friends; and as far as that principle rules in the heart, there will be constant care and effort to walk in his ways, even those that are narrow and difficult. In every instance, to keep his commandments. At all times, and in all conditions, with a sincere purpose of heart to remain devoted to the Lord, and to serve him and his kingdom among people, with all our heart, and with all our soul. This good advice is given to all; may God give us grace to take it!

Here is the care of the separated tribes to maintain their commitment to Canaan's religion. At first sight, it seemed like an attempt to set up an altar against the altar at Shiloh. God is jealous for his own institutions; we should be too, and afraid of anything that looks like or leads to idolatry. Corruptions in religion are best dealt with early on. But their prudence in following this zealous resolution is no less commendable. Many an unhappy conflict would be prevented, or quickly resolved, by investigating the matter of the offense. The memory of great sins committed previously should encourage us to stand guard against the beginnings of sin; for the way of sin is downhill. We are all concerned to correct our neighbor when he does wrong, lest we allow sin upon him. The offer made that they would be welcome to come to the land where the Lord’s tabernacle was and settle there, was in the spirit of true Israelites.

The tribes took the rebukes of their brothers well. With solemnity and meekness, they proceeded to give all the satisfaction in their power. Reverence for God is expressed in the form of their appeal. This brief confession of faith would remove their brothers’ suspicion that they intended to worship other gods. Let us always speak of God with seriousness, and mention his name with a solemn pause. Those who make appeals to Heaven with a careless “God knows,” take his name in vain; it is very unlike this. They express great confidence of their own uprightness in the matter of their appeal. “God knows it,” for he is perfectly acquainted with the thoughts and intents of the heart. In everything we do in religion, it greatly concerns us to approve ourselves to God, remembering that he knows the heart. And if our sincerity be known to God, we should also study to let others know it by its fruits, especially those who, though they misunderstand us, show zeal for the glory of God. They rejected the design of which they were suspected to be guilty and fully explained their true intent in building this altar. Those who have found the comfort and benefit of God’s ordinances cannot but desire to preserve them for their descendants, and to use all possible care that their children may be considered as having a part in him. Christ is the great Altar that sanctifies every gift; the best evidence of our interest in him is the work of his Spirit in our hearts.

It is well that there was on both sides a willingness for peace, as there was zeal for God; for arguments about religion, due to lack of wisdom and love, often prove the most fierce and difficult to resolve. Proud and irritable people, when they have placed unjust blame on their brothers, even though full evidence be brought of its unfairness, can by no means be persuaded to withdraw it. But Israel was not so prejudiced. They looked upon their brothers’ innocence as a sign of God's presence. Our brothers’ zeal for the power of godliness, and faith and love, despite fears of breaking the unity of the church, are things of which we should be very glad to be satisfied. The altar was called ED, a witness. It was a witness of their care to keep their religion pure and complete, and would witness against their descendants if they should turn from following after the Lord. Happy will it be when all professed Christians learn to copy the example of Israel, to unite zeal and steady adherence to the cause of truth with candor, meekness, and readiness to understand each other, to explain and to be satisfied with the explanations of their brothers. May the Lord increase the number of those who endeavor to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace! may increasing grace and consolation be with all who love Jesus Christ sincerely!
